question: What conditions must a Medicare beneficiary meet to qualify for home health services?
ground_truth_answer: To qualify for the Medicare home health benefit under §§1814(a)(2)(C) and 1835(a)(2)(A) of the Act, a beneficiary must: (1) be confined to the home (homebound); (2) be under the care of a physician or allowed practitioner; (3) need skilled nursing care on an intermittent basis, physical therapy, speech-language pathology services, or have a continued need for occupational therapy; (4) receive services under a plan of care established and approved by a physician or allowed practitioner; and (5) receive services from a Medicare-certified home health agency (HHA) with a valid participation agreement. Ref: 100-02, Ch.7, §30.

question: What does it mean for a patient to be 'confined to the home' under Medicare home health coverage?
ground_truth_answer: For Medicare home health purposes, a patient is considered confined to the home (homebound) when: (1) due to illness or injury, the patient has a condition that restricts their ability to leave home except with a considerable and taxing effort; or (2) leaving home is contraindicated by their medical condition. The patient may still be considered homebound if absences from home are infrequent, for short durations, or are attributable to receiving health care treatment (e.g., attending adult day care, outpatient chemotherapy, or physician visits). A physician or allowed practitioner must certify the homebound status in all cases. Ref: 100-02, Ch.7, §30.1.1.

question: What are the requirements for skilled nursing care to qualify as 'intermittent' under the Medicare home health benefit?
ground_truth_answer: Under §1861(m) of the Act, 'intermittent' skilled nursing care means care that is provided or needed on fewer than 7 days per week, or less than 8 hours per day for periods of 21 days or less (with extensions in exceptional circumstances). To meet this requirement, the patient must have a medically predictable recurring need for skilled nursing services — typically at least once every 60 days. Daily skilled nursing may be covered for diabetics who are unable to self-administer insulin, or for short periods when daily care is medically necessary, subject to documentation supporting the need. Ref: 100-02, Ch.7, §40.1.3.

question: What are the requirements for a valid home health plan of care under Medicare?
ground_truth_answer: Home health services must be provided under a plan of care that is established and approved by a physician or allowed practitioner. The plan must: (1) specify the type, frequency, and duration of services; (2) include measurable therapy treatment goals when therapy is ordered; (3) be reviewed and signed by the physician or allowed practitioner at least every 60 days; and (4) be consistent with the qualified therapist's assessment of the patient's function where therapy is involved. Allowed practitioners who may establish a plan of care include physician assistants (PAs), nurse practitioners (NPs), and clinical nurse specialists (CNSs). Ref: 100-02, Ch.7, §30.2.

question: What face-to-face encounter requirements must be met for a physician to certify a patient for home health services?
ground_truth_answer: A certifying physician or allowed practitioner must have had a face-to-face encounter with the patient no more than 90 days prior to the home health start of care date, or within 30 days after the start of care. If home health care is ordered for a new condition not evident during a visit within 90 days prior to start of care, the physician or a non-physician practitioner (NPP) must see the patient again within 30 days after admission. The encounter must be related to the primary reason for home health services. NPPs performing the encounter are subject to the same financial restrictions with the HHA as the certifying physician. Ref: 100-02, Ch.7, §30.5.1.

question: What prior hospitalization requirement must be met for Medicare to cover skilled nursing facility (SNF) services?
ground_truth_answer: To qualify for post-hospital extended care services (SNF coverage), the individual must have been an inpatient of a hospital for a medically necessary stay of at least three consecutive calendar days (not counting the day of discharge). The hospital must be either a Medicare-participating hospital or one meeting the conditions of participation for an emergency services hospital. The discharge from the hospital must have occurred on or after the first day of the month in which the individual attained age 65, or after becoming entitled to Medicare. Ref: 100-02, Ch.8, §20.1.

question: What four conditions must all be met for care in a skilled nursing facility to be covered under Medicare?
ground_truth_answer: Medicare covers SNF care when all four of the following are met: (1) the patient requires skilled nursing services or skilled rehabilitation services that must be performed by or under the supervision of professional or technical personnel; (2) the patient requires these skilled services for a condition that was treated during a qualifying inpatient hospital stay, or for a condition that arose while in the SNF for treatment of such a condition; (3) the patient requires these skilled services on a daily basis (essentially 7 days per week); and (4) as a practical matter considering economy and efficiency, the daily skilled services can only be provided on an inpatient basis in a SNF. Ref: 100-02, Ch.8, §30.

question: What is the 30-day transfer requirement for SNF coverage under Medicare, and when can it be waived?
ground_truth_answer: The beneficiary must be transferred to a participating SNF within 30 days after discharge from the qualifying hospital stay. This requirement may be waived in limited circumstances: (1) if it is medically inappropriate to begin active SNF treatment immediately and it is medically predictable that covered SNF care will be needed within a predeterminable time frame (medical appropriateness exception); (2) if the patient's medical needs and course of treatment are not predictable at the time of discharge (unpredictable needs exception); or (3) if the patient requires SNF care prior to a deferred covered treatment that was foreseeable at the time of hospital discharge. Ref: 100-02, Ch.8, §20.2.1.

question: What determines whether a nursing or therapy service qualifies as a 'skilled service' for SNF coverage purposes?
ground_truth_answer: Skilled nursing and rehabilitation services are those that: (1) require the skills of qualified technical or professional health personnel such as registered nurses, licensed practical nurses, physical therapists, or speech-language pathologists; (2) are furnished pursuant to physician orders; and (3) are reasonable and necessary to the treatment of the patient's illness or injury. A patient's diagnosis or prognosis alone should never be the sole factor in deciding a service is or is not skilled. The key issue is whether the skills of the professional are needed to safely and effectively treat the condition, or whether the services could be carried out by unskilled personnel. Ref: 100-02, Ch.8, §30.2.1.

question: What are the eligibility requirements for a Medicare beneficiary to elect the hospice benefit?
ground_truth_answer: To be eligible to elect hospice care under Medicare, an individual must: (1) be entitled to Part A of Medicare; and (2) be certified as terminally ill by a physician, meaning the individual's medical prognosis indicates a life expectancy of 6 months or less if the illness runs its normal course. Once eligible, the individual (or authorized representative) must elect to receive hospice care by filing an election statement with a particular hospice. By electing hospice, the beneficiary generally waives the right to Medicare payment for services related to the treatment of the terminal condition outside of the hospice benefit. Ref: 100-02, Ch.9, §10.

question: What must a hospice certification include to support a terminal illness prognosis under Medicare?
ground_truth_answer: For the first 90-day period, the hospice must obtain oral or written certification of terminal illness no later than 2 calendar days after hospice care is initiated. The certification must include: (1) a statement that the individual's medical prognosis indicates a life expectancy of 6 months or less if the illness runs its normal course; (2) specific clinical findings and other documentation supporting a life expectancy of 6 months or less; (3) the signature(s) of the certifying physician(s), the date signed, and the benefit period dates; and (4) for the third benefit period and beyond, a face-to-face encounter by a hospice physician or nurse practitioner occurring no more than 30 calendar days before the start of the benefit period. Clinical information must be filed in the medical record with the written certification. Ref: 100-02, Ch.9, §20.1.

question: What vehicle requirements must be met for an ambulance to qualify for Medicare coverage?
ground_truth_answer: Any vehicle used as an ambulance must be designed and equipped to respond to medical emergencies and, in non-emergency situations, be capable of transporting patients who are bed-confined or otherwise physically incapacitated. The vehicle must comply with applicable state and local laws governing ambulance vehicles, and must be equipped with items including a stretcher, oxygen equipment, emergency medical supplies, and communication systems. Ref: 100-02, Ch.10, §10.1.1.

question: What establishes medical necessity for Medicare to cover ambulance transport?
ground_truth_answer: Medical necessity for ambulance transport is established when: (1) the patient's condition at the time of transport is such that other means of transportation are contraindicated; and (2) any means of transport other than an ambulance would be medically contraindicated or would endanger the patient's health or safety. The medical necessity determination is based on the individual patient's condition at the time of the transport, not on the diagnosis alone. Documentation must be available upon request to support this determination; a physician's order alone is not sufficient proof of medical necessity. Ref: 100-02, Ch.10, §10.2.1.

question: What staffing requirements must a Basic Life Support (BLS) ambulance meet under Medicare?
ground_truth_answer: A Basic Life Support (BLS) ambulance must be staffed by a crew of at least two personnel, with at least one of those personnel certified as an emergency medical technician-basic (EMT-basic) under state or local law. The crew must meet all applicable state and local licensing and certification requirements for the vehicle type and the services performed. Ref: 100-02, Ch.10, §10.1.2.

question: What documentation is required to establish eligibility for Medicare ambulance services?
ground_truth_answer: Documentation must be available upon request that supports the medical necessity of the ambulance transport at the time it occurred. This documentation must reflect the patient's condition at the time of transport and must demonstrate why other forms of transportation would have endangered the patient's health. A physician's order for ambulance transport alone is not sufficient proof of medical necessity — the underlying clinical record must support the need. Documentation should be maintained by both the ambulance provider and the ordering physician or facility. Ref: 100-02, Ch.10, §10.2.4.

question: Are telehealth services covered under the Medicare home health benefit?
ground_truth_answer: No. Section 1895(e) of the Social Security Act governs the home health prospective payment system and explicitly provides that telehealth services are outside the scope of the Medicare home health benefit and HH PPS. This provision does not provide coverage or Medicare payment for home health telehealth services. Telehealth services furnished in connection with home health cannot be reported or billed by the home health agency as home health visits for Medicare payment purposes, and the cost of such services cannot be reported on the HHA's cost report. Ref: 100-02, Ch.7, §110.

question: What home health nursing services involving medication administration are not covered under Medicare?
ground_truth_answer: The administration of eye drops and topical ointments does not require the skills of a nurse and is therefore not covered as a skilled nursing service, even if the administration is necessary for the treatment of an illness or injury, the patient cannot self-administer, and there is no one else available to administer them. Additionally, the administration of oral medications by a nurse is generally not a covered skilled nursing service unless the complexity of the patient's condition, the nature of the drugs, and the number of drugs prescribed require the skills of a licensed nurse to detect and evaluate side effects or reactions, which must be documented in the medical record. Ref: 100-02, Ch.7, §40.1.2.4.

question: When is a patient's place of residence considered ineligible for Medicare home health services?
ground_truth_answer: A patient cannot receive Medicare home health services if they reside in an institution that itself qualifies as a Medicare provider, such as a hospital, SNF, or other participating institution. If a patient remains in a participating SNF following discharge from active care, the facility may not be considered their residence and they are not entitled to payment for home health services under either Part A or Part B. Medicare coverage is also not an optional substitute for services that a facility is required by law to provide to its patients or that are included in the facility's base contract. An individual's choice to reside in such a facility is also a choice to accept the services it holds itself out as providing. Ref: 100-02, Ch.7, §30.1.2.

question: Can venipuncture alone qualify a patient for the Medicare home health benefit?
ground_truth_answer: No. Effective February 5, 1998, venipuncture for the purposes of obtaining a blood sample can no longer be the sole reason for Medicare home health eligibility. Venipuncture alone does not establish home health eligibility. However, if a patient qualifies for home health eligibility based on a skilled need other than solely venipuncture (e.g., skilled nursing or physical therapy), then medically necessary venipuncture services may be covered as an additional service within the home health plan of care for that patient. Ref: 100-02, Ch.7, §40.1.2.13.

question: What services are explicitly excluded from the SNF Prospective Payment System (PPS) and must be billed separately?
ground_truth_answer: The following services are excluded from coverage under the SNF PPS consolidated billing rate and must be billed separately when provided to SNF residents: (1) cardiac catheterization services; (2) computerized axial tomography (CT scans); (3) magnetic resonance imaging (MRIs); (4) radiation therapy; (5) ambulatory surgery involving the use of a hospital operating room; (6) emergency services; (7) angiography services; and (8) certain other high-cost services as specified by CMS. These services continue to be billable under their respective Part A or Part B payment systems outside the SNF PPS rate. Ref: 100-02, Ch.8, §10.2.

question: Are private-duty nursing services covered under the Medicare SNF benefit?
ground_truth_answer: No. While nursing care provided by or under the supervision of a registered professional nurse is covered under SNF hospital insurance, the services of a private-duty nurse or other private-duty attendant are not covered. Private-duty nurses or private-duty attendants are those working exclusively for a particular patient. Medicare does not reimburse for individualized private-duty nursing that goes beyond the skilled nursing care included in the SNF's covered extended care services. Ref: 100-02, Ch.8, §50.1.

question: What types of supportive or personal care services provided in a SNF are not considered skilled services under Medicare?
ground_truth_answer: The following supportive or personal care services are not considered skilled services and are therefore not covered under the SNF skilled level of care benefit: (1) administration of routine oral medications, eye drops, and ointments (regardless of whether patients can be relied on to self-administer them); (2) general maintenance care of colostomies and ileostomies; (3) routine care of incontinent patients; (4) general supervision of exercises that have already been taught to the patient; (5) routine administration of medical gases after a treatment regimen has been established; (6) assistance with dressing, eating, and going to the toilet; (7) periodic turning and positioning in bed; and (8) passive range of motion exercises that can be carried out by aides or nursing personnel. Ref: 100-02, Ch.8, §30.5.

question: What Medicare coverage does a beneficiary waive when electing the hospice benefit?
ground_truth_answer: Upon electing the Medicare hospice benefit, the beneficiary waives the right to Medicare payment for any Medicare services that are: (1) related to the treatment of the terminal condition for which hospice care was elected; (2) related to a condition that is related to the terminal condition; or (3) equivalent to hospice care — except for services provided by the designated hospice (directly or under arrangement), another hospice under arrangements with the designated hospice, or the patient's attending physician if not employed by the hospice. Services for conditions that are unrelated to the terminal illness and its related conditions are not waived and may still be covered by Medicare outside of the hospice benefit, as documented in the Hospice Election Statement Addendum. Ref: 100-02, Ch.9, §20.2.1.3.

question: When is Medicare payment for ambulance services not made due to lack of actual transport?
ground_truth_answer: Medicare does not make payment for ambulance services when no actual transport of the beneficiary occurs. Specifically, no payment is made if: (1) the beneficiary dies before transport begins; (2) the beneficiary refuses transport after the ambulance crew arrives; or (3) the flight is aborted before the patient is onboard (in the case of air ambulance). Payment also cannot be made for transport of ambulance staff or others without the beneficiary onboard. The physical transport of the beneficiary from one location to another is a prerequisite for Medicare ambulance payment. Ref: 100-02, Ch.10, §10.2.6.

question: When is air ambulance transport not covered or subject to reduced payment under Medicare?
ground_truth_answer: Air ambulance transport is not covered or is subject to reduced payment in the following circumstances: (1) when transport is to a non-acute care facility such as a nursing home or physician's office — only acute care facilities are covered air ambulance destinations; (2) when the transport is to a more distant hospital based solely on patient preference, which is not a covered reason; (3) when ground transport would have been medically sufficient, in which case payment is limited to the ground ambulance rate even if air transport was used; and (4) when the flight is aborted before the patient is onboard, in which case no payment is made. Air ambulance is only covered when the patient's condition requires rapid transport that ground ambulance cannot provide, and all vehicle and crew requirements are met. Ref: 100-02, Ch.10, §10.4.4–10.4.6.

ground_truth_answer: Coverage scenario 0 is covered under Medicare Part B only if it is determined to be medically reasonable and necessary. Ref: 100-02, Ch.15, §50.

ground_truth_answer: Claim type 0 must be submitted on an 837I format or form CMS-1450. Ref: 100-04, Ch.1, §10.
